The **Saskatchewan Green Party** is a [Green](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics") [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") in [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), Canada. The party was founded as the **New Green Alliance** in 1998 by a coalition of environmental and social justice activists. In the twenty-first century, only the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Saskatchewan "New Democratic Party of Saskatchewan") and the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") have fielded more candidates than the Greens in provincial elections, although no Greens have been elected to the [Legislature](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Legislature "Saskatchewan Legislature").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The current party leader is Naomi Hunter.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Foundations and the New Green Alliance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundations and the New Green Alliance")]

In the mid-1990s, a number of environmental and social justice activists began to organize against the perceived [rightward](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics") drift of the governing New Democrats (NDP) under [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Roy Romanow](/wiki/Roy_Romanow "Roy Romanow"), who were seen to have embraced [Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way") politics.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In April 1998, organizers held a news conference, stating their intention "... to create a political party committed to protect the environment from corporate plunder and to advance a social justice agenda," before officially registering the New Green Alliance (NGA) with [Elections Saskatchewan](/wiki/Elections_Saskatchewan "Elections Saskatchewan") in January 1999.[[4]](#cite_note-NGAwebsite-4) Early on, the party debated focusing on [community activism](/wiki/Community_activism "Community activism") over [electoralism](/wiki/Electoralism "Electoralism")—future party leader Victor Lau stated in 1998 that in his opinion, "... 20 per cent of the energy of the new party should be devoted to electoral politics and 80 per cent to promoting community projects like low-cost housing, new transit systems, or solar energy."[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Others looked to revive the [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") politics of [Tommy Douglas](/wiki/Tommy_Douglas "Tommy Douglas"), the first CCF/NDP Premier of Saskatchewan, which they argued the NDP had drifted away from.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

The party's first leader was [anti-nuclear](/wiki/Anti-nuclear "Anti-nuclear") activist Neil Sinclair. In the [1999 provincial election](/wiki/1999_Saskatchewan_general_election "1999 Saskatchewan general election"), the NGA ran 16 candidates province-wide, earning 1% of the vote and approximately 4% of the vote on average where they ran.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The NGA, whose platform was characterized as "counter-ideological" in contrast to the NDP and Saskatchewan Party, who were competing to form government, found it difficult to garner media attention during the campaign.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the [2003 election](/wiki/2003_Saskatchewan_general_election "2003 Saskatchewan general election"), under the leadership of Ben Webster, the party ran 27 candidates but actually lost support compared to 1999, earning only 0.6% of the vote province-wide.

### Saskatchewan Green Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Saskatchewan Green Party")]

In 2005, the NGA changed its name to come in line with the federal Greens, becoming the Green Party of Saskatchewan; however, the party maintained that it was only loosely affiliated with the [Green Party of Canada](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada").[[8]](#cite_note-NGACBC-8) The party had several short-term leaders between 2005 and 2006, when [antiwar](/wiki/Antiwar "Antiwar") and [anti-vaccination](/wiki/Anti-vaccination "Anti-vaccination") activist Sandra Finley became leader ahead of the [2007 provincial election](/wiki/2007_Saskatchewan_general_election "2007 Saskatchewan general election"). The Greens ran a much larger campaign in 2007, fielding candidates in 48 out of 58 ridings. The party was able to capture 2.0% of the vote; Harold Johnson, running in the [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_(Saskatchewan_provincial_electoral_district) "Cumberland (Saskatchewan provincial electoral district)") riding, received 6.2% of the vote and secured the first third-place finish for the Greens.

On 6 September, 2011—the unofficial kickoff for the [2011 provincial election](/wiki/Saskatchewan_general_election,_2011 "Saskatchewan general election, 2011") campaign—Green leader Larissa Shasko resigned her position to join the campaign of [Regina South](/wiki/Regina_South "Regina South") NDP candidate [Yens Pedersen](/wiki/Yens_Pedersen "Yens Pedersen").[[9]](#cite_note-Saskatchewan_Green_Party-9) Upon her resignation, Shasko cited frustrations with the "inner workings" of the party and concerns about a potential candidate.[[9]](#cite_note-Saskatchewan_Green_Party-9) Her resignation triggered a visit to Saskatoon by federal Green leader [Elizabeth May](/wiki/Elizabeth_May "Elizabeth May"), who also announced that deputy leader Victor Lau had been elevated to become the provincial party's new leader[[9]](#cite_note-Saskatchewan_Green_Party-9)—a decision that was reinforced by party members at a special leadership convention held on 25 September.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Lau and the party ran a full slate of 58 candidates—a first for the party—43% of which were women.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Although no Green candidates were elected, the party increased its vote share and emerged from the election as the third largest party in the province, surpassing the Liberals for the first time.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

After the 2011 election, the party began an effort to organize on a constituency basis across the province. In March 2015, the party was rebranded as the Saskatchewan Green Party and ran a nearly full slate again in the [2016 provincial election](/wiki/2016_Saskatchewan_general_election "2016 Saskatchewan general election") under Lau, though fell back in vote share across the province, finishing in fourth place.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In March 2020, former federal Green candidate Naomi Hunter was named leader of the Greens ahead of the [2020 provincial election](/wiki/2020_Saskatchewan_general_election "2020 Saskatchewan general election").[[14]](#cite_note-HunterRL-14) In the election, the party increased its vote share, finishing with its second highest total after 2011, but again finished fourth, this time finishing behind the fledgling [Buffalo Party](/wiki/Buffalo_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Buffalo Party of Saskatchewan").[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15) The party saw its vote share decline in the [2024 election](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_general_election "2024 Saskatchewan general election"), although it again finished in fourth place overall.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
